73
   E.g. Oesterrieth, Urheberrecht, 173. (One notes with bemu sement that within the second category of
public figures described in this text, “professors and academics” rank second after politicians, and ahead of
“writers, artists, virtuosos, actors.” Ah Germany!)
74
   Bertrand, Droit à la vie privée, 26-32.
75
   Sehring, Presserecht, 432 (members of aristocracy not ipso facto public figures, but peculiarly liable to
become public figures on account of their activities or accomplishments), 437.
76
   Cour d’Appel de Paris, 1ère chambre, section B, Arret du 20 Septembre 2001, Barcia c/S.A. Groupe
Express.

24
 of “society” privacy in continental law. Over the past several generations, the basic

commitment to control of one’s public image has been extended well beyond its origins

in the problems of the Princess Carolines of the world, in ways that do indeed affect the

lives of ordinary people. This is most especially true of the areas where the conflicts

between continental and American norms are most heated: areas like consumer data,

credit reporting, public nudity and the dignity of criminal offenders. These are all realms

of life in which continental law has forcefully extended privacy protections to non-

celebrities and non-royals: Control of one’s “image,” in the continental mind, now

includes not only everybody’s right to keep their names out of the newspapers, but

everybody’s right to control the use of their consumer data and the like as well;

everybody’s right to privacy in the workplace; everybody’s right (if they should need it)

to respectful imprisonment; and more. Continental privacy law is, as it were, “society”

privacy for everybody.

One recent German popular guide to the law proudly puts it in this way in its

opening pages, in language that deserves to be underlined. Privacy rights, the book

explains, are part of a larger law of the “defense of personal honor”; and nowadays it’s

not just the “royal houses” whose image is threatened; “everybody is in danger.”77 Silly

enough—but also truly appealing in its way. Of course it matters to insist that everybody

counts the same way royalty does, from racial minorities and prison inmates on up

through the ranks of society. Of course it matters.

77
  Peter Raue, Persönlichkeitsrechte. Die Verteidigung der persönlichen Ehre (Frankfurt a.M.: Fischer,
1997), 11-12. For a similar view from an American with a continental sensibility, see Rosen, Unwanted
Gaze, 202: “private citizens run the risk of being treated like celebrities in the worst sense, vilified rather
than celebrated. . . “

25
                                              IV

There is no better way to grasp this continental social ideal than to trace its

historical development, from its origins in the nineteenth-century world of dueling,

through the Nazi period, and on into its modern forms of workplace privacy. Continental

jurists have also always tried to understand “privacy” as a species of personal honor. As
